top_gaps: How an inbound feedback submission is routed to a specific organization (end_users has no org link; no submission endpoint/form/token mechanism specified).
The role-based authorization model: owner/admin/agent are defined but no acceptance criterion enforces who may triage, create releases, or comment.
Feedback status transition rules: UC-002 AC-4 privileges 'new' but the legal transition graph is otherwise undefined.
Enforcement home for guard rules (UC-006 AC-2 'no changes once shipped', UC-003 AC-4 'at most one release') — DB trigger vs application layer, and neither is live-tested.

Booking lifecycle rules (accept-only-when-requested, complete-only-when-confirmed, rate-only-when-completed) are stated in ACs but enforced nowhere in the model; the agent must invent CHECK constraints, triggers, or app guards — and the live tests are inconsistent about whether these are DB errors or WHERE-clause no-ops.

UC-006/DA-2 contradicts its own AC-2: the AC says rating a non-completed booking SHALL be rejected, but the live test inserts that rating and expects success (rowcount=1). The agent gets opposite signals from the same use case.
stars 1-5 range has no CHECK constraint and no validation home despite being stated in glossary and UC-006.
Payment.amount has no derivation rule — UC-003 initializes a pending payment but never says how the amount is computed (hourly_rate x time window? fixed?).
'Provider/customer notified' steps and 'update provider reputation' / rating-propagation NFR have no entity, field, or integration — reputation aggregation and notifications are unhomed.
Capacity enforcement and the enroll-vs-waitlist routing rule are never specified — the agent must guess how 'full' is detected and whether UC-001 rejects when at capacity.
Offer lifecycle timing: expires_at has no concrete duration and the 'expired' status has no handling/re-offer policy.

Re-enrollment contradiction: dropped enrollment rows are kept but unique(student_id,course_id) blocks re-enroll via waitlist; usecases.md ('error ~ check') and usecases.sql ('error ~ unique') disagree on UC-005/DA-3.
Waitlist_entry has no terminal state after a successful accept, and the drop->offer / accept->enroll policies are asserted but only tested as isolated inserts, not as enforced causation.

Status-transition legality is undefined: ordered 'new→triaged→planned→shipped→declined' workflow in the brief vs. enum-only validation in UC-008 — agent must guess whether to enforce transition guards.
Owner-uniqueness ('exactly one owner per org') is asserted in the glossary but enforced nowhere; UC-004 lets admins freely set the owner role.
Invitee vs. removed member states both collapse to is_active=false with no distinct 'pending'/'invited' field, so UC-002's pending entry and UC-005's soft-delete are indistinguishable.
end_users has a global-unique email and no organization_id, making end-user identity cross-tenant — intended per UC-006 but its visibility consequences across orgs are unspecified.
Pricing rule on acceptance is unspecified — UC-005 'sets the agreed price' and the test hardcodes a value; the agent must guess hourly_rate x hours vs. provider-entered.

Authorization/ownership is undefined — nothing ties the accepting/declining Provider to the service, the completing Customer to the booking, or the rating's Customer to the booking; the agent must invent who-may-act rules.

Status-transition and rating-eligibility rules are labeled 'error ~ check' but require triggers (a plain CHECK cannot see the prior status); resolved only because model.dbml Notes explicitly say triggers enforce them.
Seat-offer lifecycle is undefined: waitlist_entries has no offered/expires_at state, and UC-005 explicitly leaves 'mark as offered OR create offered-seat record' unresolved
Offer expiry is promised in scope but has zero specification (no timeout, state, or AC)
'Course is full' / capacity enforcement is a brief assumption with no UC, AC, or DB constraint — nothing blocks enrolling past capacity or requires fullness before waitlisting
The drop->offer->accept->enrollment causation is only a flow policy; no AC or data assertion mechanizes it, and 'auto-offer' (scope) contradicts the Registrar-driven manual accept in FL-006/UC-006
UC-006/DA-2 tests a student foreign-key error instead of the missing-waitlist-entry rejection its AC-2 specifies
Authorization: hierarchical owner/admin/agent privilege model asserted in the brief but no UC, AC, or live test enforces role-based denial — the agent must invent the authz layer.
Transport/API surface unspecified (no endpoints, payloads, REST-vs-GraphQL), so the agent picks the external interface unilaterally.
No use case for browsing/listing/filtering the inbox (only single-item history view) — primary read/query/pagination surface left to invention.
Physical end-user submission channel (widget/public endpoint/email) named as the intake path but left out of scope, so ingestion wiring is undefined.

Booking lifecycle transition guards live only at the application layer and are never specified -- legal status moves (requested->confirmed/declined, confirmed->completed) and the rate-only-completed-bookings rule must be invented; the schema enforces uniqueness/CHECK/FK but not transition legality.
Price-at-acceptance rule is left open: A5/UC-005 say it is 'derivable from hourly_rate x window' but 'stored as an explicit amount', so the agent must choose compute-vs-free-entry.
No transport/auth/API surface is specified -- fine for the stated data-of-record scope, but a full app build would invent it.
Offer expiry is completely unspecified — no expires_at/timeout column, no rule for what happens to an unaccepted offer, no re-offer to the next waiting entry.
Capacity/'full' is narrative only: occupancy is never defined and UC-001 enroll has no capacity guard, so 'a course is full' (UC-004) is unenforceable as written.

Flows vs. use cases contradict on causation: FL-005/FL-006 make drop->offer and accept->enroll automatic policies, but the use cases and usecases.sql implement them as independent manual registrar UPDATEs/INSERTs.
Lifecycle transitions are unguarded — nothing requires offer only from waiting or accept only from offered, and accept->enroll is neither atomic nor links the new enrollment to the waitlist entry.
